story:
Time: 10:19 p.m., South Florida. A red light glares forever, daring me to wait. The road is empty for miles. I'm James Montgomery—glasses, brown hair, sweater vest, a ten-year local journalist who learned Spanish mostly to survive the neighborhood. I'm not eager for this family reunion, except for the galettes: my mother's holiday-only cookies, nine eggs and a pound of sugar. I flick a green-glinted bug off my window, press the gas, and run the light.

Down the wooded driveway, my childhood house crouches behind a porch lamp burning a hypnotic red. The paint peels, the porch boards gap; by night the place looks like a grinning skull. Alex's huge black truck blocks the front, still bragging with ancient "Class of 2002" and "State Champions 2001" stickers. The door says, "God bless this home and all who enter." I tell myself it'll be fine.

Inside, nothing has changed: the hulking entertainment center, cat towers everywhere, the worn brown carpet turning the living room into an obstacle course. I call for Mom. A cold draft knifes my neck. I turn and nearly collide with Alex—grinning too wide. His salesman uniform is stained dark red; his long blond hair is slicked back; a bruise swallows his left eye. His trademark class ring is missing.

"Want to spend some more time together, Alex?" I joke.

He giggles—high and wrong, a hyena-scratch of a laugh—then goes back to staring. He actually calls me "James," which he never does. He says everyone's waiting downstairs.

In the kitchen: a feast, untouched. Then the prize—galettes, golden, waffle-scored, exactly as I remember. I bite one. Deceptively good. Footsteps pound up from below; the basement door flies open. Alex, still grinning: "Everybody is waiting downstairs. Shame you're so late." A shovel leans by the doorframe. No sign of anyone else.

"Are you sure they're down there?" I ask.

"Oh, they're down there. All of them. Every part of them," he breathes.

He won't go first. "Go ahead," he says softly.

I descend. The basement glows faintly with the same stubborn red as the stoplight and porch lamp. I remember the time he locked me down here when we were kids. "Mom? Guys?" No answer. The floor feels wrong. In the half-light I see a fresh-dug hole ringed by mounds of dirt. I lean closer—and feel a presence behind me.

Headlights bloom: a truck rushes straight at me from the far end of the basement, as if there's a hill inside the concrete. I flinch; the truck melts into nothing. A figure bursts through the dying glare and shoves me.

I fall hard. Metal and bone grind beneath me. The smell confirms what my hands already know. On a detached hand, something gleams: Alex's class ring.

Above, the giggle again. "Comfy?" it asks.

It looks like Alex, but Alex is in pieces below. My voice shakes. "You're not Alex. What are you?"

Its eyes bulge out, then re-form as glowing green reptile slits. Spines erupt along its back; a slick tail unfurls. It's the same creature that had been mimicking Alex all along—something that copies people, wears them, and dumps the remains here.

It lifts the shovel and starts to bury me. Dirt thuds down, faster, heavier. The red light thins, then disappears. The air shrinks. I think of the light I ran, the reunion I meant to dodge, the cookies I wouldn't leave, the house that felt almost—but not quite—like mine. I look at my watch: 11:47.

Nearly two hours.

Already buried.
